coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of mesoporous nanoprobe with molecularly imprinted fluorescent carbon dots embedded within silica network for the selective and sensitive detection of aspirin in ground water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Mohiuddin, Irshad
Singh, Raghubir
Kaur, Varinder
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a highly selective and sensitive protocol has been developed to detect aspirin (ASP) by using mesoporous silica nanoprobe equipped with molecular imprints and fluorescent carbon dots (ms-MIP-FCDs). The fabrication of nanoprobe is based on the reprocessing of rice straw to fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) and their amalgamation with molecular imprinting within mesoporous silica network (ms). The molecular imprints for ASP were introduced via sol gel process. The nanoprobe displayed spectrofluorimetric signal due to photoinduced electron transfer between ms-MIP-FCDs and ASP. The synergistic action of mesopores, molecular imprints, and FCDs makes the spectrofluorimetric recognition of ASP quick (in 5 min), sensitive (LOD = 1.16 nM), and selective in the presence of potential competing interferents (such as salicylic acid, methyl salicylate, salsalate, 5-methylsalicyldehyde, naproxen, ketoprofen, diclofenac, mefenamic acid, carbamazepine, and levofloxacin). Furthermore, the method can be successfully applied to detect the ASP from ground water samples with the recoveries in the range of 94.1–104.2% and good precision (relative standard deviation (RSD) < 6.0%). The prepared fluorescent probe also displayed excellent performance with enhanced reusability/durability.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of mesoporous nanoprobe with molecularly imprinted fluorescent carbon dots embedded within silica network for the selective and sensitive detection of aspirin in ground water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh-160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh 160011, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, DAV College, Sector 10, Chandigarh-160011, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a highly selective and sensitive protocol has been developed to detect aspirin (ASP) by using mesoporous silica nanoprobe equipped with molecular imprints and fluorescent carbon dots (ms-MIP-FCDs). The fabrication of nanoprobe is based on the reprocessing of rice straw to fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) and their amalgamation with molecular imprinting within mesoporous silica network (ms). The molecular imprints for ASP were introduced via sol gel process. The nanoprobe displayed spectrofluorimetric signal due to photoinduced electron transfer between ms-MIP-FCDs and ASP. The synergistic action of mesopores, molecular imprints, and FCDs makes the spectrofluorimetric recognition of ASP quick (in 5 min), sensitive (LOD = 1.16 nM), and selective in the presence of potential competing interferents (such as salicylic acid, methyl salicylate, salsalate, 5-methylsalicyldehyde, naproxen, ketoprofen, diclofenac, mefenamic acid, carbamazepine, and levofloxacin). Furthermore, the method can be successfully applied to detect the ASP from ground water samples with the recoveries in the range of 94.1–104.2% and good precision (relative standard deviation (RSD) < 6.0%). The prepared fluorescent probe also displayed excellent performance with enhanced reusability/durability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin or ASP) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medicine that can control acute inflammation and cardiovascular diseases [1,2]. The salicylic acid produced due to metabolism of ASP is responsible for pharmacological activity however it shows hazardous effects when present above 2.2 mM concentration [3]. The excessive use of ASP if present as environmental residue, may lead to psychotomimetic effects, gastroduodenal ulcers, and hepatic or renal lesion [4-8]. Therefore, development of new analytical methods based on liquid chromatography [9-11], spectrophotometry [12-14], spectrofluorimetry [15-17], and Raman spectroscopy [18-20] for the analysis of ASP is of great interest. These approaches produce quantifiable data with excellent results. However, many of them are difficult to handle, expensive, and require specialised individuals to operate the instruments and analyse the results.

Sensing based analytical techniques have piqued the interest of researchers owing to their simplicity, high sensitivity, fast response, low cost, ease of operation, and possibility for real-time analysis. Moreover, use of green precursors to fabricate a sensor, especially a nanoprobe, is current area of interest. Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) are gaining popularity as sensing nanoprobes due to their exceptional photoluminescent properties, ease of preparation, high water solubility, minimal cytotoxicity, high thermal stability, and remarkable photo-stability [21]. FCDs are superior fluorophores than other materials like quantum dots and organic dyes which also have toxicity and photo-bleaching concerns [22]. The intriguing features of FCDs are ideally suitable for sensing a variety of chemical entities ranging from simple organic molecules to complicated structural frameworks [23]. However, the principal challenges that limits the applicability of FCDs-based sensors is their non-selectivity towards a particular analyte. Therefore, the combination of FCDs with molecular imprinting is the excellent approach to increase their selectivity towards target species. Molecular imprinting is a technique in which molecular imprints are generated in the three dimensional network to allow the interaction of a particular species with the surface [24]. The inherent durability of mesoporous silica-based imprinting matrices is an effective way to introduce selectivity in the FCDs for the target. Moreover, the mesoporous nature of the material lowers the mass transfer resistance and increase the rate of binding between surface and the analyte [25,26]. The compatibility of FCDs with the silica network provides benefits to generate three dimensional cavities. Another advantage of using FCDs as probes is the natural origin of their precursors. The FCDs can be generated by hydrothermal carbonization of rice straw, chitosan, orange peels, and coffee grounds [27-31]. The so generated FCDs have been successfully applied to recognize various chemical species in biological and environmental samples [27-31]. Rice is the world's second most significant cereal crop, and its cultivation creates massive volumes of trash in the form of straw and bran [32]. Recently, hydrothermal carbonization of rice straw is proved as an eco-friendly, facile, and classical route for the synthesis of FCDs in aqueous media.

Herein, a nanoprobe having molecular imprints for ASP and FCDs (ms-MIP-FCDs) embedded within mesoporous silica have been fabricated from the rice straw and is employed for the determination of ASP in ground water samples. It is important to mention that contamination of ground water is the crucial concern as its remediation is difficult. It is the first report for the determination of ASP by using ms-MIP-FCDs as spectrofluorimteric probe. It offers unique merits as compared to conventional sensors like environmental friendly precursors, selective binding of ASP due to imprints, quick response, reusability, cost-effective, and practicality.

ASP, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), naproxen (NAP), ketoprofen (KET), diclofenac (DCF), mefenamic acid (MEF), carbamazepine (CBZ), levofloxacin (LEV), salicylic acid (SCA), methyl salicylate (m-SCE), salsalate (SSE), and 5-methylsalicyldehyde (m-SAE) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and ammonium hydroxide were obtained from SRL Pvt. Ltd (Maharashtra, India). Buffer solutions with pH 3–11 were used in the fluorescence experiments aimed at investigation of pH influence on ASP binding to the surface of ms-MIP-FCDs. Chemicals used for the preparation of buffer solutions with different pH values like disodium hydrogen phosphate/citric acid (pH = 3–5), potassium dihydrogen phosphate/dipotassium hydrogen phosphate buffer (pH = 5–7), potassium dihydrogen phosphate/sodium hydroxide buffer (pH = 8–11) were procured from Merck group private Ltd. The total ion concentration (TIC) was taken into account when making the buffers, as this might affect the interaction of ASP with the ms-MIP-FCDs (Table S1). The TIC was calculated using the equation stated below (Eq. 1):

Distilled water was used for all the experiments and was prepared in the laboratory by using the distillation unit (Mode LA621, Elga). Rice straw was obtained from a local paddy field (Pinjore, Haryana, India). The rice straw was washed thoroughly three times with distilled water to remove any impurities. Subsequently, the rice straw was soaked in distilled water for around 6 hrs. The rice straw was then dried (60 °C for 1 hr) and ground for use in subsequent experiments to synthesize FCDs.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu Fluorescence RF-6000 equipped with a quartz cell. The slit widths of both the excitation and emission monochromators were set at 10 nm with an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. JascoV-530 UV-Vis spectrophotometer was employed for recording the absorption spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (in neat solid-state) was performed to assess the chemical functionality of ms-MIP-FCDs probe by obtaining vibrational spectra in the 4000–400 cm-1 range with a Thermo-scientific NICOLET IS50 FT-IR and Perkin Elmer RX-I FT_IR spectrometers. Morphological characteristics of the synthesized probe were acquired with a JEOL JEM 2100 Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging. The elemental mapping of ms-MIP-FCDs probe was investigated by using energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) (Model JEM 2100 plus, JEOL, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out with a SDT Q600 (V20.9 Build 20) instrument (Artisan Technology Group, Champaign, IL) and the results were recorded from 20° to 1000 °C under a dry nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min-1. The dinitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and structure parameters (including specific surface area, pore-volume, and pore radius) were determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analyzer (Microtrac Belsorp Mini-II, Bel, Japan, Inc). pH measurements were recorded on a Cole-Parmer P200–02 Series Benchtop pH meter (Cole-Parmer Pvt Ltd, Mumbai India). An Eppendorf centrifuge 5804 R (Hamburg, Germany) was used which can be rotated at a speed of up to 14,000 rpm, temperature ranges from − 9–40 °C, and operating at a power consumption of max. 1650 W.

The FCDs were synthesized from rice straw as a source of carbon using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.20 g of rice straw was added to the 40 mL of distilled water under continuous stirring. The suspension was then transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 hrs. The resulting dark brown suspension was collected from the autoclave at room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (−5 °C) for 15 min to remove any suspended large particles with a size > 50 nm. The supernatant solution was purified using the membrane filters (0.22 µm) and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

1.00 mL of FCDs solution was added to 10 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 10 min 200 μL of APTES (functional monomer) and 40 mg of ASP (template) were added to the mixture and the contents were stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of CTAB surfactant (0.2 M in water) was added in the above reaction mixture under continuous stirring conditions. Afterwards, 800 μL of TEOS (cross-linker) and 1.5 mL of ammonia solution (as catalyst) were added in the mixture. The suspension was again stirred for another 12 hrs in the dark. The formed product was subjected to centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 15 min) and washed with a mixture of methanol/CH3COOH acid (9:1, v/v) to remove ASP and CTAB. The obtained ms-MIP-FCDs nano-probe was dried in an oven (at 60 °C for 1 hr). In addition, mesoporous silica based non-imprinted FCDs (ms-NIP-FCDs) were also prepared by following the same procedure but without using ASP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard solutions and detection of ASP
ce:section.ce:para: Quality assurance protocol must be established to ensure efficiency and reliability of the analytical results. Before use, all laboratory glassware was washed carefully with methanol, acetone, and distilled water and then dried at 70 °C for 3 hrs and covered in clean aluminium foil. Each analysis of procedural blanks, real samples, and spiked samples was performed in triplicate. A 1.0 mmol L-1 standard stock solution of ASP was prepared by dissolving standard sample in methanol. Stock solutions were kept in screw capped vials (5 mL) and placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The desired working standards of required concentration were prepared by conducting multi-step dilutions of the standard stock solutions in distilled water to achieve the final concentration (Table S2).

To a 5 mL volumetric flask, 2.5 mg of MI-FCDs, 500 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.0, TIC = 2.07 × 10-2 M) prepared by using 55 mM potassium dihydrogen phosphate and 44 mM dipotassium hydrogen phosphate, and 1 mL of ASP solution were added. The volume was made to 5 mL with distilled water. After incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 510 nm with an excitation wavelength of 410 nm.

The amount of nanoprobe was optimized by taking 5 mL solution of ms-MIP-FCDs containing different amounts in the range 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700 mg L-1 and 5.55 nM of ASP was added. The solution was kept for 5 min and fluorescence response was recorded at 510 nm (λexc = 410 nm) at room temperature. Similarly, 5 mL solution containing 500 mg L-1 ms-MIP-FCDs and 5.55 nM of ASP was prepared at different pH values and spectrofluorimetric response was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample handling and extraction from ground water
ce:section.ce:para: The ground water samples were collected from five different locations of Punjab, India including ground water sample 1 (Maur), ground water sample 2 (Mansa city), ground water sample 3 (Burhan Bhatti, Jalalabad), ground water sample 4 (Baga Purana), and ground water sample 5 (Muktsar city). The water samples were collected in polyethylene bottles which were soaked in 10.0% HNO3 acid (65.0% ultrapure, Himedia) for 24 hrs, washed twice with millipore water and rinsed thrice by water to be sampled. The water was collected from a depth ranging between 60 and 240 ft. Before doing any further sample pre-treatment, all the collected ground water samples were investigated by the ms-MIP-FCDs probe for ASP presence, so as to show the applicability of the reported probe under real world conditions. However, ASP was not detected or was below the method detection limit in the ground water samples.

Sample preparation is a crucial bottleneck in the whole analytical process, especially for the ultra-trace quantitation of chemicals in environmental samples. An adequate sample preparation helps to improve accuracy and minimise the risk of results distortion. To this end, ultrapure nitric acid was used to preserve the water samples by bringing down the pH below 2. Furthermore, all the collected water samples were filtered through Whatman filter paper (pore size 11 µm) and nylon-6,6 membrane filters (pore size 0.2 µm) to remove the suspended particles before analysis and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Recovery experiments were conducted with spiked concentrations of ASP via standard addition approach to validate the accuracy and practicality of the synthesized ms-MIP-FCDs probe. 2.5 mg of ms-MIP-FCDs (or ms-NIP-FCDs) were dispersed into 5 mL of 100-fold diluted water samples spiked with various concentrations of ASP (5.55, 27.57, and 55.55 nM). The mixture was kept as such for 5 min at room temperature with gentle shaking. Finally, solutions of each sample were collected and analyzed by using the as-synthesized ms-MIP-FCDs. All the experiments were repeated three times to calculate the deviation.

For reliable quantification of the target ASP in complex matrices, ten-point matrix calibration were also carried out in concentration ranging from 1.66 to 194.27 nM of ASP after being spiked in all the ground water samples (Table S3). Notably, the matrix matched calibrations did not pass through the origin because the blank sample was not included in the calibration plot obtained from the standard addition approach.

A matrix effect (ME) is considered to be confounding factor that may suppress or enhance the analyte response in the quantitation of environmental contaminants. Thus, it hampers the accuracy and trueness of the developed method to affect the practicality of the method. ME in each of all ground water samples was studied by comparison between calibration curves prepared in distilled water and those obtained in matrix matched samples after spiking at different tested concentrations. ME for each target ASP in different matrices were calculated as: %ME = [(slope matrix/slope solvent)− 1] x 100. Positive values represent signal enhancements induced by matrix, while negative values indicate suppressions of the analyte signal. ME can be considered significant when |ME= >20.0% [33]. As %ME in real samples are less than 20.0% (Table S3), there is no significant ME. Therefore, calibration standard in distilled water were used for the quantification of target ASP during the method validation and have been employed in real sample analysis.

The design of probe is based upon the amalgamation of FCDs, molecular imprinting, and mesoporous silica. In the first step, rice straw was transformed to FCDs by hydrothermal process for obtaining FCDs from green precursors. The surface of FCDs consists of various functional moieties such as –COOH and –OH that provide active sites to generate three dimensional networks. In the second step, FCDs were imprinted by sol-gel process in which APTES was used as a functional monomer, CTAB as structure-directing surfactant, TEOS as cross-linker, and NH4OH as a catalyst. The triethoxysilyl moiety of APTES was hydrolyzed to produce silanol groups during the process. These silanol groups were further condensed with the –COOH, or –OH, or –NH2 groups of FCDs to form covalent bonds. Further, the dissociation constant (pKa) value of ASP is 3.5. Under neutral conditions, roughly one end, i.e., aminopropyl part of functional monomer interacted with the ASP via hydrogen bonding. Major part of the ASP is ionised so as the carboxylic acid group become a resonance structure. Therefore, in addition to hydrogen bonding, carboxylate group interacts electrostatically with the protonated amino-propyl group to form an acid-base pair. Further, a three dimensional network of silica was generated around FCDs, functional monomer, and ASP by adding TEOS. During polymerization, CTAB was added to generate the mesopores in the silica network so that the interaction sites can become accessible during adsorption. The ASP and CTAB were removed from the conjugate which generated cavities for ASP adsorption and mesopores to facilitate the movement of chemical species inside the material. The removal of ASP template from three dimensional networks was easy due to non-covalent interactions between template and functional monomer. The fabrication of ms-MIP-FCDs probe is shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical properties of the synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The structural information of FCDs and ms-MIP-FCDs was furnished by FT-IR, HR-TEM, EDS, N2 adsorption and desorption measurements, and TGA. FT-IR was conducted to assess the nature of functional groups present on the surfaces of the fabricated materials ( Fig. 2). In the FT-IR spectrum of FCDs, the broad band at 3255 cm-1 is attributed to O–H stretching vibration. The absorption bands at 1707 cm-1 and 1160 cm-1 are vibrations as ascribed to CO stretching and C-O stretching. In the FT-IR spectrum of ms-MIP-FCDs, the peaks observed at 1055 and 782 cm 1 signify asymmetric stretching vibrations of SiOSi and the bending vibration of the SiO groups, respectively, indicating the formation of silica network. The bands located at 3438 cm 1 and 1630 cm-1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH, respectively. Hence, the FT-IR analysis confirms the successful incorporation of APTES in the network.

The morphological and topographical characteristics of FCDs, ms-MIP-FCDs, and ms-NIP-FCDs were analyzed using HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 3 (a-b), FCDs are well separated from each other and have uniformly dispersed spherical particles without apparent aggregation. The surface is seen smooth with an average diameter of 7.69 ± 1.70 nm. The FCDs (dark spots) are distributed with the polymer matrix of ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NIP-FCDs and display rough polymeric surface with a size of 25–30 nm (shown in Fig. 3 (c-f)). Furthermore, SAED patterns of FCDs and ms-MIP-FCDs show diffused rings indicating the amorphous phase of the nanoparticles in both FCDs and ms-MIP-FCDs (Fig. 3 g and h).

The EDS spectrum of the FCDs (Fig. S1a) was obtained to track the presence of C and O in the synthesised material. The data shows successful formation of FCDs revealing C and O as elements in ms-MIP-FCDs. In addition to C and O, there were additional peaks due to N and Si which also provides strong evidence for the formation of desired product (Fig. S1b).

N2 adsorption and desorption measurements were conducted at 77 K to investigate the specific surface areas and pore size distribution of the synthesized materials as per the BET theory and BJH method. The shape of the isotherm exhibits a typical type IV pattern (IUPAC nomenclature) with an H3-type hysteresis loop, which is the characteristic of mesoporous materials ( Fig. 4 a). From the isotherm, the sharp increase in N2 adsorption at partial pressure p/po of 0.30–0.45 has been observed, indicating uniform size of the mesopores. The pore size distribution pattern of ms-MIP-FCDs is shown in Fig. 4 b and peak pore diameter is found to be 2.2 nm. The BET surface area has been found 309.18 m2 g−1, with the pore volume being 0.35 cm3 g-1. This type of structure reduces the mass transfer resistance and increases the site accessibility.

The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was assessed by TGA (Fig. S2a and b). TGA profile of FCDs shows 96.0% mass loss when temperature is raised from room temperature to approximately 160 °C due to the removal of water molecules, and organic functional groups present on the surface of FCDs (Fig. S2a). In the TGA curves (Fig. S2b), three mass loss ranges are observed for ms-MIP-FCDs. The first mass loss of 14.0% below 150 °C is likely a reflection of loss of residual or adsorbed water molecules. The second region of 26.6% ranging from 150° to 300 °C can be attributed to the thermal decomposition of the polymer layer. The third region of 15.4% ranging from 350° to 1000 °C can be ascribed to the decomposition of APTES. The percentage of silica in the probe was found 44.0%. The TGA profile clearly confirmed the good thermal stability of the nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence emission spectra and possible sensing mechanism of ms-MIP-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the developed probe, the FCDs were embedded within the mesoporous structured imprinted silica layer, so the main challenge was whether the ms-MIP-FCDs would retain their fluorescent properties or not. Interestingly, ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NIP-FCDs showed similar fluorescence spectra to their parent FCDs with a little decrease in the emission intensity and slight red shift in the emission maximum (Fig. S3). The red shift could be attributed to the formation of a hybrid structure i.e., ms-MIP-FCDs or ms-NIP-FCDs which could increase the effective size of the FCDs and reduce the quantum size effect [26]. The fluorescence quantum yield was noted relative to the standard solution of perylene in ethanol (QY = 0.92). The quantum yield of FCDs and ms-MIP-FCDs was found to be 0.64 and 0.53, respectively.

The ms-MIP-FCDs nanoprobe was used to selectively recognize and detect the ASP. The ms-MIP-FCDs exhibited quenching of the emission signal centred at 510 nm in the presence of traces of ASP. It is believed that photo-induced electron transfer (PET) from the excited state of the FCDs to the ASP is responsible for the quenching of emission signal. In ms-MIP-FCDs, fluorescence arises due to radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, a green emission was observed at 510 nm (λexc = 410 nm). When ASP is added in the solution, it interacts with the amino group present in the binding sites of ms-MIP-FCDs via hydrogen bonding. Major part of the ASP with pKa value of 3.5 is ionised under neutral conditions. Thus, in addition to hydrogen bonding carboxylate group interacts electrostatically with the protonated amino-propyl group to form an acid-base pair. This interaction significantly restrain the radiative recombination of the electron hole pair [34]. The UV-Vis absorption band of ASP is close to the band gap of the FCDs (Fig. S4a). The electrons present in the conduction band of FCDs can jump to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of ASP molecules. This transfer of electrons to the LUMO of ASP can lead to the fluorescence quenching of FCDs. Conversely, this energy transfer is not possible during fluorescence quenching as there is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of ASP and emission spectrum of FCDs (Fig. S4b). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching is caused by the PET quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of analytical parameters for ASP detection
ce:section.ce:para: ms-MIP-FCDs as a fluorescent probe has been employed to successfully detect target ASP from ground water samples. In order to get an optimal detection of ASP by ms-MIP-FCDs, the key experimental parameters (e.g., dosage amount, incubation time, and solution pH) were studied to maximize detection efficiency of the probe. All optimising parameters were evaluated using the fluorescence quenching efficiency in terms of the fluorescence intensity difference (Fo − F); here, Fo and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a given concentration of ASP. The mean values were calculated for each test based on triplicate analyses. The following experimental conditions were found to give optimal results: (a) Optimal dosage amount: 500 mg L-1; (b) incubation time: 5 min; (c) solution pH: 7 (Table S4-S6). Detailed information regarding optimization of the ms-MIP-FCDs for ASP sensing is provided in the supplementary information (Section S1 of SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity studies of ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NI-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing ability of ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NIP-FCDs was investigated by recording emission response in the concentration range 1.66–194.27 nM of ASP ( Fig. 5a and b). The change in fluorescence intensity of ms-NIP-FCDs was significantly smaller than the ms-MIP-FCDs with the increasing concentration of ASP. The higher quenching efficiency of ms-MIP-FCDs likely reflects the effective binding with ASP due to the presence of specific cavities for ASP.

To confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching data were analyzed according to the Stern Volmer equation (Eq. 2):
(2)

K

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a given concentration of ASP, respectively. KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and [ASP] is the concentration of quencher (ASP). A plot of F0/F versus [ASP] determines the KSV value. The Stern-Volmer constants (KSV) with their standard errors for ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NIP-FCDs were estimated as 0.011 ( ± 7.2 × 10-4) and 0.0015 ( ± 8.7 × 10-5), respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), the Stern-Volmer plots for ms-MIP-FCDs/ms-NIP-FCDs are non-linear indicating the nature of the quenching mechanism is not dynamic [35]. This non-linear relationship of sensing in solution indicates the quenching mode may be a combination of static and dynamic quenching.

Consequently, the obtained experimental data were fitted to Lehrer equation (the modified Stern-Volmer equation) to better understand the interaction mechanism [35]. The Lehrer equation is expressed by Eq. 3:

[ ASP ]
Where, fa is the fraction of accessible fluorescence. The obtained Lehrer plots are linear ( Fig. 7(a) and (b)) for both ms-MIP-FCDs and ms-NIP-FCDs, and the intercept of the plot provides the 1/fa parameter, while the constant Ka is calculated as the ratio of intercept/slope. All of the derived Lehrer parameters were summarized in Table S7. The value of fa and Ka for ms-MIP-FCDs are 0.742 and 0.0692 ( ± 1.1 × 10-1), respectively. As depicted in Table S7, the KSV values calculated from the linear fit of F0/F versus [ASP] are relatively small as compared to Ka calculated from Eq. 3 in ms-MIP-FCDs. It was evident that new species are not formed in the mixture as no shifts in the fluorescence emission maxima of ms-MIP-FCDs (λem = 510 nm) were observed after the addition of ASP [36,37]. Thus, the fluorescence quenching must be the result of electron transfer processes [36].

To learn more about the binding affinity of the fluorophores towards the quencher (ASP), we estimated the binding parameters such as binding constant (KB) and the number of interaction binding sites (n). The values of KB and n were determined by using the Scatchard Eq. (4) [38]:
(4)
log
F
o
− F
F
= log
K
B
+ n log
[ ASP ]

Based on the plot of the log [(F0-F)/F] versus log [ASP], the values of n and K were obtained from slope and Y-intercept, respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 8(a) and (b), and the corresponding binding parameters for the fitting are summarized in Table S7. The comparison of KB indicates that the values for the ms-MIP-FCDs (0.0451 ± 0.03) are higher than those for the ms-NIP-FCDs (0.0175 ± 0.05). Accordingly, it can be inferred that the ms-MIP-FCDs exhibit higher binding affinity towards ASP compared to ms-NIP-FCDs. Further interpreting the results, that gives a conclusion that n values are nearly equal to 1 for ms-MIP-FCDs signifying there is only one preferable binding site present in ms-MIP-FCDs for ASP.

The imprinting factor (I.F.) defined in Eq. (5), was used to evaluate the selectivity of the ms-MIP-FCDs as sensing probe. The I.F. and standard deviation of I.F. (
σ
I . F .
) was calculated by Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively:

Under optimum conditions (i.e., 500 mg L-1 as the optimum quantity, incubation time of 5 min, and pH = 7), the I.F. was calculated to be 8.375 ± 0.64 which indicated that presence of specific cavities in ms-MIP-FCDs produced selective determination of ASP.

Aiming at evaluating the feasibility of the probe on complex matrices, the selectivity of ms-MIP-FCDs for ASP was investigated by taking similar structural analogues (e.g., SCA, m-SCE, SSE, and m-SAE) and other competing interferents (e.g., NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, CBZ, and LEV). Different potential interferents including SCA, m-SCE, SSE, m-SAE, NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, CBZ, and LEV were added to monitor the fluorescence quenching. The chemical structures of these compounds are displayed in Fig. S5. The results are graphically shown in Fig. S6. It can be seen that the change in fluorescence intensity of ms-MIP-FCDs upon ASP rebinding was considerably high (Fig. S6). The interfering molecules including SSE, m-SAE, NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, CBZ, and LEV cause little fluorescence quenching as comparison to ASP. However, the quenching efficiency is higher in case of SCA and m-SCE (structural analogues) but is still lower than ASP. The reason for high selectivity is the presence of abundant recognition cavities over the surface of ms-MIP-FCDs that are compatible with ASP in shape, size, and functional groups rather than other molecules. Hence, the prepared ms-MIP-FCDs exhibited stronger fluorescence response and higher affinity for ASP during adsorption. However, the interfering molecules with different shapes and molecular weights than ASP were not complementary to the recognition sites. As such, they were unable to quench the ms-MIP-FCDs fluorescence, although they were able to interact with ms-MIP-FCDs by H-bonding. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of ms-NIP-FCDs was also investigated in the presence of these analytes. Since, there were no recognition sites that were completely complementary to the size, shape, and functional group of ASP in ms-NIP-FCDs, so it was difficult to interact with ASP effectively. Noticeably, compared with ms-MIP-FCDs, the fluorescence change of ms-NIP-FCDs was also relatively weak in the solutions containing ASP or its analogues, because the interaction between the two was through non-specific physical adsorption. This suggested that ms-NIP-FCDs could not effectively bind either ASP or other drugs indicating its poor selectivity.

Based on the experimental data from Fig. S6, the point values for the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) can be calculated using Eqn 2. The plot of the obtained KSV values of ms-MIP-FCDs versus ms-NIP-FCDs is seen in Fig. 9. The ms-NIP-FCDs exhibit the similar Ksv values to ASP and its analogues (e.g., SCA, m-SCE, m-SAE, SSE, NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, and CBZ), which indicate no specific recognition sites over its surface. The Ksv values for SCA and m-SCE is approximately same as for other compounds on to ms-NIP-FCDs, while it shows a somewhat higher value over ms-MIP-FCDs. A possible reason for the latter results can be the structural similarity between SCA, m-SCE, and ASP (Fig. S5). The ms-MIP-FCDs exhibit larger Ksv values than the corresponding ms-NIP-FCDs for ASP, reflecting the highest quenching (sensing) effect. This value corresponds to the highest reported value and it is closer to the X-axis, compared to the first bisector. This observation signifies the selective sensoring capacity of ms-MIP-FCDs. Advantage of this specific graph is that fluorescence data, in the form of bars (as presented in Fig. S6), are replaced by data points and it is readily visible that which compound is selectively measured [39].

In addition, the effects of possible interferents including potential interfering drugs (e.g., NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, and CBZ), cations (e.g., Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Cu2+), anions (e.g., Cl‐, SO4 2–, NO3 ‐, and CO3 2–), and some organic compounds (e.g., urea, uric acid, and ascorbic acid) that can be present in real samples along with the target, i.e., ASP were tested under optimal conditions. Hence, fluorescence analysis was performed after adding the interfering species (at varying concentration levels) to an ASP solution as described in Section 2.4. The tolerance limit was taken as the concentration of added interfering species that can cause the variation of ± 5% to the fluorescence signal. As summarized in Table S8, most species including cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Cu2+) and anions (Cl‐, SO4 2–, NO3 ‐, and CO3 2–) had no significant effect on the analysis of ASP even when they were present at 500 times greater concentrations than ASP. The results confirmed that the presence of intermediate (100-fold NAP, KET, DCF, MEF, CBZ, urea, ascorbic acid, and uric acid) did not affect the determination of ASP. The results demonstrate that the interferences are unlikely to exert much effect on the sensing of ASP by ms-MIP-FCDs at the tested concentration range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility, repeatability, and analytical performance of the ms-MIP-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the synthesized ms-MIP-FCDs was investigated by performing time dependent fluorescence emission study and no significant change was found in the emission response of the ms-MIP-FCDs over 120 min (Fig. S7). This also revealed that ms-MIP-FCDs were uniformly dispersed in the solution without obvious settlement. The stability of probe was evaluated by storage at 4 °C for three weeks. The intensity was found to be 90.4% of its initial value after three weeks. The variance and the results of the analysis are shown in Fig. S8 (days 1 through 21). The fluorescence emission intensity for the first nine days of testing were contributing 99.1% (n = 1) to 98.5% (n = 9), respectively. For the later days (i.e., 10–21), the ms-MIP-FCDs probe maintained the fluorescence emission intensity upto 90.4%. The corresponding %RSD values for the duration of the study ranged from 1.74% to 3.4%. The variation in fluorescence intensity is negligible during 3 weeks, indicating the physical stability and chemical inertness of ms-MIP-FCDs probe due to their highly stable cross-linked polymer network.

To ensure reproducibility, ms-MIP-FCDs were prepared under the same conditions as mentioned in subsection 2.3.1 at three different times. Each batch of the developed ms-MIP-FCDs was then used to detect ASP under the optimized experimental parameters (mass concentration = 500 mg L-1; incubation time = 5 min; pH = 7), followed by fluorometric analysis (Table S9). The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were between 1.4% and 3.1%. These results indicated excellent batch to batch reproducibility and consistency in the data using this sensing system.

Reusability of ms-MIP-FCDs is one of the momentous factors for its practical application. This reduces the cost of the materials and protocols. The reusability of the ms-MIP-FCDs was analyzed up to 14 removal-rebinding cycles. In the first cycle, the detection was conducted within 5 min by following the optimized procedure (i.e., 500 mg L-1 as the optimum quantity and pH = 7). The ms-MIP-FCDs probe was recovered by washing with methanol/acetic acid solvent (to remove ASP). The recovered ms-MIP-FCDs were poured into fresh solution of ASP for the second cycle of detection and so on. As the number of cycles increased, the response of ms-MIP-FCDs was slightly decreased which may be due to slight loss of imprinting cavities in each cycle (Fig. S9). However, the material retained over 80.4% of its original fluorescence intensity up to the seventh cycles indicating good reusability of ms-MIP-FCDs. The recovery values obtained over seven extraction cycles of ASP ranged from 98.0% (1st cycle) to 80.4% (7th cycle). The decline in its intensity were seen in the range of 78.5% (8thcycle) to 70.4% (12th cycle) due to the saturation of surface-active sites or the loss of probe after multi-run experiments. Furthermore, the effect of the presence of co-existing interferents on ASP emission intensity (as discussed in Section 3.5) after 12th cycle was also investigated. The tolerance limit was determined as the concentrations of concomitant substances (%RSD < 5). However, a sharper decrease in fluorescence intensity from 70.4% (12th cycle) to 24.8% (14th cycle) was observed for ASP at the end of the 14th reusable cycle. This sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity of ms-MIP-FCDs can be ascribed to pore blockage or sudden collapse of already wash-away imprinted polymer network and FCDs, with additional load of interferents. Overall, the results indicate that ms-MIP-FCDs has good reusability with strong chemical and mechanical stability.

The analytical performance of the developed material was evaluated in terms of linearity, limit of detection limit (LOD), and intra-day precision. The ms-MIP-FCDs exhibited linear response for ASP in the concentration range of 1.66–194.27 nM with a coefficient of determination (R2) value 0.98. The LOD of 1.16 nM which was determined from the plot of fluorescence intensity as a function of concentration (See Fig. S10) using the relation of 3δ/b, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and b as the slope of calibration curve. Under the optimized condition, the computed relative standard deviation (RSD) value of < 6.0% indicated good measurement repeatability.

To investigate the practical utility of the proposed ms-MIP-FCDs probes in real sample analysis, the developed method was applied for quantitative detection of ASP in ground water samples. However, ASP was not detected or was below the method detection limit in the ground water samples. Hence, the suitability of the method in these matrices was determined by measuring their analytical recovery after adding different amounts of ASP at different concentration levels (i.e., 5.55, 27.75, and 55.51 nM) based on the standard addition method. Analysis data for the ASP in real samples are summarized in Table 1. As shown in Table 1, the recovery values of ASP from ground water samples were in the range of 94.1–104.2% with RSD < 6.0%. These results indicate the substantial and reliable accuracy of our method for the detection of ASP in environmental water samples including ground water.

The performance of the proposed method was examined by comparing the results with those obtained from other reported methods such as spectrofluorimetry [40-42], electrochemical methods [43-46], and high-performance liquid chromatography [47,48]. As can be seen in Table S10, ms-MIP-FCDs exhibited improved analytical results relative to other methods of ASP detection. Among the previously mentioned sensors/methods used for detecting ASP, N-doped carbon quantum dots decorated copper oxide modified glassy carbon electrode (NCQD/Cu2O/GCE) was identified as the best probe in terms of LOD [44]. It offered LOD value of 2 nM for target aspirin with recoveries of 96.3–102.7%. The LOD value in our study (e.g., 1.16 nM) is the lowest as compared to all data sets summarized in Table S10. Moreover, ms-MIP-FCDs offered good recoveries of 94.1–104.2% against target ASP confirming its superiority over NCQD/Cu2O/GCE. Secondly, from an economic standpoint, the reusability of the sensor was good enough to allow reuse up to 7 times without obvious decay in sensing ability maintaining the substantially high stability and recovery (upto 80.4%). These properties are responsible for its excellent cycling performance. In addition, the rice straw used for the synthesis of ms-MIP-FCDs by a simple approach, is a readily available bio-waste for which sensing applications may offer a synergistic win–win solution for simultaneous waste management and environmental monitoring. Thus, ms-MIP-FCDs-based sensing method offers a viable workaround for the construction of highly effective and efficient sensing devices that are economically and environmentally feasible for the rapid implementation of remedial measures.

The scale-up approach with cost analysis will provide further insight into the economic aspects related to the production phase of ms-MIP-FCDs for the selection of sensor for practical applications. The cost estimation performed to determine economic feasibility of waste based ms-MIP-FCDs. Rice straw prices were not included because it is assumed that rice straw is an abundant agricultural waste. The cost of the chemical reagents used for the synthesis of the ms-MIP-FCDs were considered: APTES (USD 5.04 ×103/ton), ASP (USD 1.26 ×102/ton), CTAB (USD 2.4 ×103/ton), TEOS (USD 1.70 ×103/ton), Ammonia (USD 1.26 ×102/ton), MeOH (USD 2.65 ×102/ton), and CH3COOH (USD 0.50 ×102/ ton). The economic assessment for the production of ms-MIP-FCDs is given in Table S11. The estimation was based on the cost incurred by the raw material, chemicals (APTES, ASP, CTAB, TEOS, ammonia, MeOH, and CH3COOH), distilled water, and power consumption (including magnetic stirring, sonication, autoclave synthesis, centrifugation, and drying). Referring to Table S11, the production cost for ms-MIP-FCDs were calculated as USD 887.32/ton. As such, this approach confirms that ms-MIP-FCDs is economically viable for designing cost efficient portable devices for real world applications.

The quantitative assessment on environmental impact of present ms-MIP-FCDs synthesis methodology was assessed in terms of green and circular metric like environmental-factor (E-factor). The E-factor elucidates eco-friendly performance of a chemical process at bulk scale production. E-factor is the amount of waste produced considering all process components, including reactants and solvent, yet excluding water, relative to the mass of the desired product [49,50]. A higher E-factor means more waste is generated and causes risk of negative environmental impact. The ideal E factor is zero. A lower E-factor value of present ms-MIP-FCDs synthesis process (i.e. 0.746) implies less waste generation and more green approach (for detailed calculations please refer to section S2 of SI). Accordingly, the results revealed that the above synthetic process is benign with respect to environmental parameters and production of ms-MIP-FCDs by this process follows the green pathway.

An innovative fluorescent sensing system based on the ms-MIP-FCDs has been designed to enhance the detection efficiency and accuracy for ASP. The FCDs were prepared by hydrothermal process using rice straw as a green precursor. The molecular imprints for ASP and FCDs were incorporated in the mesoporous silica via sol-gel process using ASP as a template, APTES as a functional monomer, CTAB as a structure-directing surfactant, and TEOS as a cross-linker. The constructed ms-MIP-FCDs probe exhibited excellent fluorescence response to ASP through the imprinted recognition effect and PET effect. The developed nano probe was suitable for the determination of ASP in ground water samples. It offers advantages of rapid recovery, long-term stability, high sensitivity and selectivity, easy preparation and low cost, eco-friendly, and moisture resistance. The low cost of the ms-MIP-FCDs as sensor (e.g., USD 887.32/ton) may make it a better choice among the existing options for the sensing-based methods. This strategy would furnish the potential application of the developed ms-MIP-FCDs probe in the realm of environmental and biological monitoring.
